card.intuition: A set of four properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably.
card.analogy: An ATM withdrawal: either the machine gives you cash AND deducts it from your balance, or neither happens. It's impossible for it to give you cash but forget to update the balance.
when_to_use: Any financial or sensitive system where data integrity is more important than raw speed
When multiple users are writing to the same pieces of data simultaneously
Evaluating a new database technology (SQL vs NoSQL trade-offs)
gotchas: Achieving full ACID compliance often requires locking data, which can slow down high-traffic systems
Durability doesn't mean the server can't fail; it means once a transaction is committed, it won't be lost even if the power cuts out immediately after

steps.label: Begin Txn
steps.annotation: Transaction starts — DB records a begin marker in the WAL.

steps.label: Execute
steps.annotation: Multiple reads/writes run. Changes held in memory — not yet committed.

steps.label: Validate
steps.annotation: DB checks constraints, foreign keys, and isolation rules.

steps.label: Commit
steps.annotation: Changes durably written to disk (Durability). Visible atomically (Atomicity).

steps.label: Rollback
steps.annotation: Any failure reverts ALL changes — database returns to prior state (Atomicity).

exercise.prompt: Match the ACID property to its definition.
exercise.test_code:
assert identify_property("All or nothing") == "Atomicity"
assert identify_property("Committed data is permanent even after crash") == "Durability"
assert identify_property("Concurrent transactions don't interfere") == "Isolation"
print("PASS")
